name: Madrigal de la Vera y Embalse Rosarito — Sierra de Gredos
region_intro: Mountain biking around Candeleda offers diverse terrain, transitioning from the plains of the Tiétar River to the foothills of the Sierra de Gredos. The region features extensive trail networks, including routes along several gorges fed by mountain waters and through dense forests of chestnut, ash, and alder trees. This varied landscape provides options for different skill levels, from forest tracks to technical descents. Best mountain bike trails around Candeleda The most popular mountain bike trail is Embalse 'El Rincón' Trail, a 28.1 miles (45.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 35 minutes to complete, offering views of the reservoir and surrounding natural areas. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Madrigal de la Vera y Embalse Rosarito — Sierra de Gredos, a moderate 17.3 miles (27.9 km) path. This route explores the area around the Rosarito Reservoir and the lower slopes of the Sierra de Gredos. Local mountain bikers also love the Arbillas Viewpoint – Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge loop from Candeleda, a 43.7 miles (70.3 km) trail leading through varied landscapes including viewpoints and river crossings, often completed in about 6 hours 41 minutes. Mountain biking around Candeleda is defined by the dramatic Sierra de Gredos foothills, scenic gorges, and lush forests. The network offers options for easy rides on forest tracks as well as challenging technical trails and downhill descents. The routes in Candeleda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 500 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Candeleda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are there in Candeleda?
A: Candeleda offers a wide selection of mountain bike trails, with over 40 routes available. These trails cater to various skill levels, from easy forest tracks to challenging technical descents.

Q: Are there mountain bike trails suitable for beginners or families in Candeleda?
A: Yes, Candeleda has 9 easy mountain bike trails that are great for beginners or families. These routes often follow simpler forest tracks, providing a relaxed experience through the region's lush forests. For example, routes around the Rosarito Reservoir offer diverse terrain and scenic views without extreme difficulty.

Q: What kind of challenging mountain bike trails can I find in Candeleda?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, Candeleda features 6 difficult mountain bike trails. These routes include demanding technical trials and significant elevation gains. The area around El Raso, a part of Candeleda, is known for its dedicated downhill descents. A challenging route to consider is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40317342" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arbillas Viewpoint – Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge loop from Candeleda&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 70 km and includes varied landscapes and river crossings.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in Candeleda?
A: Yes, many of the mountain bike routes in Candeleda are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20133724" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente de la Zúa – Santuario de Chilla loop from Candeleda&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a substantial ride through varied terrain.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the mountain bike trails in Candeleda?
A: The trails in Candeleda often pass through stunning natural landscapes. You can explore several gorges, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7005777" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gredos Gorge&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4780196" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pinar Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, fed by crystalline waters from the Sierra de Gredos. The region also features dense forests of chestnut, ash, and alder trees, and the scenic Rosarito Reservoir, which is a crucial wintering ground for common cranes.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or historical sites near the mountain bike routes?
A: Yes, Candeleda's trails can lead you past several notable landmarks. You might encounter the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5678370" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aquelcabos Medieval Bridge&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2840984" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to the Iberian Ibex at Nogal del Barranco&lt;/a&gt;. Further afield, you could visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3986954" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Arenas de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6316610" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Bridge of Madrigal de la Vera&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Candeleda?
A: Candeleda is suitable for mountain biking year-round, thanks to the establishment of the 'Candeleda-Gredos Natural Sports Park'. However, the spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for riding. Summers can be warm, while higher altitudes in winter may experience snow, offering different challenges.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy the most about mountain biking in Candeleda?
A: The mountain biking experience in Candeleda is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Riders frequently praise the diverse terrain, which transitions from the plains of the Tiétar River to the foothills of the Sierra de Gredos, offering everything from easy forest tracks to challenging technical trails and scenic views of gorges and reservoirs.

Q: Are there any specific routes that offer great viewpoints?
A: Many routes in Candeleda provide excellent viewpoints due to the region's dramatic elevation changes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21951768" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arbillas Viewpoint – Guisando Village loop from Candeleda&lt;/a&gt; is specifically named for its viewpoint, offering panoramic vistas of the surrounding landscapes, including the Sierra de Gredos.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the mountain bike trails in Candeleda?
A: While the region is very natural, specific rules for dogs on mountain bike trails can vary. It's generally advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in protected natural areas or near wildlife. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you clean up after them to preserve the natural environment.

Q: Is there parking available at the trailheads in Candeleda?
A: Parking availability can vary by trailhead. Candeleda is a popular outdoor destination, and many trail access points, especially those leading to well-known areas like the Rosarito Reservoir or the Sanctuary of Chilla, typically have designated parking areas. It's recommended to check specific route details for parking information.

Q: Are there any mountain bike routes that pass by cafes or places to eat?
A: While specific routes might not directly pass cafes on remote sections, many trails start or end in Candeleda or nearby villages like Madrigal de la Vera. These towns offer various cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options where you can refuel before or after your ride. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1379801544/madrigal-de-la-vera-y-embalse-rosarito-sierra-de-gredos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Madrigal de la Vera y Embalse Rosarito — Sierra de Gredos&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, connects to Madrigal de la Vera.
tips: Known as the "raso" pond, it is worth going to the Los Riveros bridge to have a drink/eat something at one of the tables or bathe in the river while enjoying the landscape that surrounds us.
Passing point on the road where it is worth slowing down to admire the alpine Gredos mountain range.

The Rosarito Reservoir, located between the provinces of Ávila and Toledo, near Oropesa, is an ideal natural setting for outdoor activities, birdwatching, and rural tourism.
Hiking and cycling: There are peaceful routes like the CM-5150 and trails overlooking the reservoir.

The Roman bridge of Madrigal, also known as the "Old Bridge," is a bridge of Roman origin that crosses the Alardos Gorge, a narrow valley carved by the Tiétar River. Currently, it consists of only one arch; its original form is unknown, as it underwent several reconstructions.
The bridge was built as part of the Roman road that linked Plasencia with Puerto del Pico and Ávila.
Nice observation point from which you can see the town of Madrigal de la Vera and the Sierra de Gredos.

Mechanical point with tools and pump (next to the school).
Fountain.
Small grocery store.
Bar.
